question: Will the Democratic Party win the SC-03 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the SC-03 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: South Carolina's 3rd Congressional District, with its R+17 partisan voting index, drives the 92% trader consensus for a Republican House election win, bolstered by GOP nominee Sheri Biggs' decisive June primary victory after incumbent Jeff Duncan's retirement announcement. Recent Winthrop University polling showed Biggs leading Democrat Byron Smythe 54%-28%, aligning with nonpartisan forecasts rating the seat safe Republican amid limited Democratic infrastructure in the Upstate region. Early voting has begun without major catalysts, reflecting historical GOP dominance in similar districts. Realistic challenges include a sudden Biggs scandal or overwhelming national Democratic turnout, though base rates from past cycles suggest low probability of flipping.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. William Timmons' strong hold on South Carolina's 4th Congressional District, rated R+15 on the Cook Partisan Voting Index, anchors trader consensus at 89.5% for a GOP win, reflecting his 2022 margin of 62% and consistent double-digit victories in prior cycles. The Democratic nominee, Michael McGaugh, trails as a decided underdog in this reliably red Upstate seat encompassing Greenville and Spartanburg. Recent primary results solidified Timmons' nomination after a competitive GOP runoff in June, with no major scandals or polling shifts emerging ahead of early voting; national headwinds for Democrats further suppress upset odds, aligning market pricing with historical base rates for safe Republican districts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. Ralph Norman holds a commanding lead in South Carolina's 5th Congressional District, a reliably red seat with a strong GOP baseline, fueling trader consensus at 89% for the Republican Party. Recent polls from September show Norman ahead by 25-30 points over Democratic challenger Kate Burr, bolstered by his unchallenged primary victory and fundraising edge exceeding $1 million. The district's R+12 partisan lean per Cook Political Report reinforces this outlook, with no major scandals or shifts altering the trajectory ahead of the November general election. Democrat odds at 10.5% reflect long-shot status amid minimal competitive polling movement.

eventMetadata.context_description: South Carolina's 6th congressional district House race shows trader consensus heavily favoring Democrats at 91.5%, driven by the seat's longstanding Democratic dominance—longtime Rep. James Clyburn's stronghold with Biden capturing 75% in 2020 and Clyburn winning 73% in 2022. The June primaries solidified nominees: Democrat Michael B. Moore, a state representative with Clyburn's backing who prevailed in a tight runoff, versus Republican Stewart Jones, a lesser-funded state lawmaker. Sparse polling reinforces this edge in the majority-minority district (D+20 Cook PVI). Challenges could arise from a national Republican wave lifting turnout or unforeseen candidate scandals, though historical base rates suggest low upset risk absent major shifts before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Rep. Russell Fry's (R) incumbency and dominant 64% victory in 2022 anchor trader consensus at 89.5% for Republican success in South Carolina's 7th Congressional District House race, bolstered by the seat's R+8 partisan lean where Donald Trump carried it by 13 points in 2020. Recent GOP primary results saw Fry easily dispatch challengers, while Democrat Barbara Jo Kamara advanced unopposed, but without competitive fundraising or polling to close the gap—Fry holds a multimillion-dollar cash advantage. National House dynamics favoring Republicans further solidify the tilt, with no major catalysts shifting odds ahead of the November general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. Dusty Johnson's substantial polling leads, often exceeding 50 points over Democrat Colby Janssen, anchor the market's 91.5% implied probability for a GOP hold in South Dakota's at-large House seat. The state's deep Republican lean, reflected in its R+16 Cook Partisan Voter Index and consistent double-digit Trump victories, bolsters this frontrunner status, alongside Johnson's fundraising dominance and clean record absent major scandals. Trader consensus prices in minimal upset risk, though scenarios like a sudden Johnson health issue, felony conviction, or anomalous Democratic turnout surge in this safely red district could narrow odds, albeit with low historical precedent in similar races.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus overwhelmingly favors the Republican candidate in Tennessee's 1st Congressional District House race, reflecting the seat's entrenched GOP dominance with a Partisan Voting Index of R+24 and incumbent Diana Harshbarger's strong primary win in August. Recent developments, including forecasters like Cook Political Report rating it Solid Republican and minimal Democratic fundraising by challenger Kevin Jenkins, reinforce this lopsided outlook amid scant competitive polling. While national Democratic turnout surges or unforeseen Harshbarger scandals could theoretically narrow the gap, such shifts remain improbable in this reliably red district ahead of the November general election.

This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the TN-08 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican David Kustoff holds a commanding lead in Tennessee's 8th Congressional District House race, with trader consensus at 92.5% implying a near-certain victory amid a district rated R+15 by partisan indexes. Recent August primaries saw Kustoff win unopposed while Democrat Heath Shuler advanced against weak opposition, reinforcing historical blowouts—Kustoff's 2022 margin exceeded 35 points. Superior fundraising, with Kustoff raising over $1 million versus Shuler's modest haul, and lack of competitive polling further solidify GOP dominance in this West Tennessee stronghold. Realistic challenges include a major Kustoff scandal or unforeseen Democratic national surge, though both remain low-probability given current trajectories and upcoming early voting trends.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Steve Cohen's commanding lead in Tennessee's 9th Congressional District, a reliably blue urban seat encompassing Memphis with a strong African American voter base, drives the 93.5% trader consensus for a Democratic hold. Recent polling from sources like FiveThirtyEight shows Cohen ahead by 30+ points over Republican challenger Timothy Bearden, bolstered by Cohen's fundraising dominance and 17-term incumbency since 2006. The district's D+24 partisan lean reinforces this edge, with no major shifts from the June primaries. Realistic challenges include a sudden Cohen scandal, health issue, or overwhelming national Republican wave boosting turnout, though historical base rates for such safe seats suggest low upset risk.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Texas's 1st congressional district, a solidly Republican stronghold with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+22, trader consensus heavily favors the GOP at 92.5% implied probability, driven by incumbent Nate Moran's strong 2022 victory margin of 73% and the district's consistent support for Republican presidential candidates, including over 70% for Trump in 2020. No recent polls show competitiveness, with Democrat Roy Martinez Jr. trailing far behind amid low fundraising and visibility. Moran's unopposed primary and clean record reinforce this edge. Realistic challenges include a major scandal or unforeseen national Democratic surge, though historical base rates for such safe seats suggest stability ahead of November's election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Texas's 2nd congressional district, a solidly Republican seat with a partisan voting index of R+14, anchors trader consensus at 88% for the GOP in the House election winner market, driven by incumbent Dan Crenshaw's strong reelection bid. Crenshaw secured 64% in the March Republican primary against challengers like Jameson Ellis, while Democrat Jrmar Jefferson advanced unopposed in his primary, facing long odds amid the district's history of 20+ point GOP margins in presidential races. Recent fundraising reports show Crenshaw with over $1 million cash-on-hand versus Jefferson's modest totals, and no public polling indicates a competitive contest. National GOP momentum for House control further bolsters the implied probability, with the November 5 general election as the next catalyst.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Keith Self's strong hold on Texas's 3rd Congressional District, a reliably conservative North Dallas suburb with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+14, anchors trader consensus at 88.5% for the GOP in the House election winner market. Self's 2022 victory margin exceeded 30 points, bolstered by the district's affluent, low-turnout demographics favoring Republicans. Democrat Michelle Jenkins, a retired Army colonel, trails significantly in fundraising and early indicators, with no recent polls narrowing the gap amid national GOP enthusiasm post-primaries. Absent major scandals or wave shifts, forecasters like the Cook Political Report rate TX-03 as Safe Republican, tempering Democratic odds at 10.5% despite broader House battleground dynamics.

eventMetadata.context_description: Texas's 4th congressional district, a Republican stronghold with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+22, drives the heavy trader favoritism toward the Republican candidate at 86%, reflecting the incumbent advantage of Rep. Pat Fallon, who cruised unopposed through the March GOP primary. Fallon's prior victories—72% in 2022—underscore the district's consistent conservative lean, where Donald Trump carried over 75% in 2020. The Democratic nominee, Simon Dakpe, advanced unopposed but faces long odds in this low-profile race rated Solid Republican by nonpartisan forecasters. No recent polls, scandals, or shifts have altered sentiment ahead of the November ballot, sustaining the lopsided market consensus.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Jake Ellzey holds a commanding lead in Texas's 6th Congressional District, a solidly Republican seat with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+16, driving trader consensus to 88% for the GOP in the 2024 House race. Ellzey secured 63% in 2022 and cruised through the March GOP primary with 78% against minor challengers, facing Democratic nominee Darryl Anderson, who won a low-turnout primary. No recent polls exist, but nonpartisan ratings from Cook Political Report and others label it "Solid Republican," reflecting Trump's 2020 margin of over 30 points here. Absent major scandals or national wave shifts, traders price minimal upset risk ahead of the November 5 general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Rep. Lizzie Fletcher's commanding lead in Texas's 7th Congressional District House race drives the 91.5% trader consensus for Democrats, anchored by her 20-point 2022 victory margin in a D+7 seat Biden carried by 15 points in 2020. All major forecasters rate it Solid Democratic, with Fletcher unopposed in her primary and facing underfunded GOP challenger John Gutierrez amid weak Republican recruitment. No recent polls or catalysts indicate a shift, reflecting incumbency advantage and Houston-area fundamentals. Realistic challenges include a national GOP wave boosting turnout or unforeseen Fletcher scandal, though base rates for such upsets in safe seats remain low.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Michael McCaul's longstanding dominance in the safely Republican TX-08 district drives the 89.5% trader consensus for a GOP victory, reflecting the area's strong conservative lean—Trump carried it by over 30 points in 2020 and McCaul won re-election in 2022 with 68% of the vote. Democrat Elizabeth Wray, the nominee after an uncontested primary, trails significantly in fundraising and lacks competitive polling data. Recent primary results in March solidified McCaul's position without surprises, while his role as House Foreign Affairs Committee chair bolsters his profile amid steady national GOP momentum. No major catalysts have emerged to challenge these probabilities ahead of the November general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Michael McCaul's entrenched position in the safely Republican 10th Congressional District of Texas anchors the 87.5% trader consensus for a GOP House election win. The district, encompassing Austin suburbs and rural areas east of the city, carries a strong GOP partisan lean—evident in 2020 results where Trump prevailed by double digits—and earns "Solid Republican" ratings from nonpartisan forecasters like the Cook Political Report. McCaul, chair of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, dominates fundraising with over $1 million cash-on-hand versus Democrat Jody Jenkins' modest totals, following his unchallenged primary. Recent catalysts include robust Republican early voting turnout across Texas and McCaul's internal polls showing a 25+ point lead, amid a national landscape favoring GOP House retention.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Veronica Escobar holds a commanding position in Texas's 16th congressional district, a reliably blue El Paso-area seat with a D+19 partisan lean where Biden won by over 30 points in 2020, driving the 92.5% trader consensus on a Democratic victory. Recent Emerson polling shows Escobar leading Republican Randy Sivic 58%-31%, bolstered by her superior fundraising and unchallenged primary dominance amid limited GOP resources. This reflects the district's Hispanic-majority demographics favoring Democrats on border and economic issues. Realistic challenges include a major Escobar scandal, unprecedented Republican turnout surge in a midterm cycle, or abrupt polling shifts, though none have materialized amid quiet campaign news.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Jodey Arrington's commanding lead in Texas's 19th Congressional District, a reliably red seat with a Cook Partisan Voting Index of R+25, drives the 91.5% trader consensus for a GOP hold, reflecting historical blowout margins like his 2022 73% victory amid weak Democratic challengers. eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the TX-21 House race strongly favors the Republican Party at 87.5%, reflecting incumbent Chip Roy's dominant position in this solidly Republican district spanning Austin suburbs, San Antonio exurbs, and Hill Country areas. Recent early voting trends show high GOP turnout, with no major catalysts shifting odds ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Republican traders' 60.5% consensus for the TX-23 House seat favors incumbent Tony Gonzales (R) due to the district's R+5 partisan lean, where Trump won by five points in 2020, and Gonzales' consistent mid-single-digit leads in recent polls like the October RMG Research survey showing him up 48%-42% over Democrat Santos Limon. Gonzales overcame a tight March primary runoff amid backlash for bipartisan votes, bolstering his resilience, while superior fundraising—$1.4 million cash-on-hand versus Limon's $200,000—supports GOP ground efforts. Early voting underway in Texas could amplify turnout edges, though Limon's retail politics keeps Democrats viable at 37.5%, reflecting district competitiveness amid national House battleground dynamics.

eventMetadata.context_description: Beth Van Duyne's incumbency and consistent double-digit polling leads anchor the 80% trader consensus for a Republican hold in Texas's 24th Congressional District House race. The GOP incumbent secured victory by 25 points in 2022 and holds a Solid Republican rating from forecasters like Cook Political Report, reflecting the district's conservative North Texas suburbs around Fort Worth. Recent Emerson College polling shows Van Duyne ahead 53%-40% against Democrat Steven Woodrow, bolstered by superior fundraising and GOP national tailwinds. Early voting turnout aligns with 2022 patterns favoring Republicans, with no major shifts despite national Senate focus, positioning Democrats' 19.5% odds as a long-shot upset.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the Texas 27th Congressional District House race strongly favors the Republican Party at 87.5%, reflecting incumbent Michael Cloud's entrenched advantage in this solidly Republican seat with a partisan voting index of R+9. Cloud secured a decisive 66% in the March GOP primary, facing Democrat Teresa Champion, who holds minimal fundraising or polling edge amid the district's conservative South Texas demographics around Corpus Christi. Recent Cook Political Report ratings confirm "Solid Republican" status, with no major shifts from early voting turnout or national headwinds altering the outlook, positioning a GOP hold as the baseline expectation ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the Texas 29th congressional district House race strongly favors the Democratic Party at 93%, driven by incumbent Rep. Sylvia Garcia's entrenched position in this heavily Democratic Houston-area seat with a D+28 partisan voter index. Garcia cruised to an easy Democratic primary win with over 70% of the vote, facing a low-profile Republican challenger amid minimal GOP investment in this safe blue district. Recent polling, where available, shows Garcia leading by 30+ points, reflecting the area's majority-Hispanic electorate and urban lean. Realistic challenges would require a major Democratic scandal, unprecedented Republican turnout surge, or national red wave—scenarios undermined by historical base rates in similar districts and current national House dynamics. Election Day remains the key catalyst.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Jasmine Crockett holds a commanding lead in Texas's 30th Congressional District, a reliably Democratic seat with a D+27 Cook Partisan Voter Index, driving trader consensus to 91.5% for the Democratic Party. Recent polls show Crockett ahead by 30+ points, bolstered by her dominant fundraising—over $1 million raised versus under $100,000 for Republican challenger James Rodgers—and the district's urban Dallas demographics favoring Democrats. Historical election margins exceeding 40 points reinforce this edge. Realistic challenges include a major Crockett scandal, Rodgers momentum from unexpected endorsements, or a national Republican wave, though none appear imminent ahead of early voting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on the Texas 31st congressional district House race strongly favors the Republican nominee at 85.5%, reflecting the district's R+9 partisan lean where Donald Trump won by 11 points in 2020, coupled with incumbent John Carter's retirement after dominating prior cycles. GOP primary winner Brian Dunn, a former prosecutor, leads Democrat Abigail Anastas in recent polls like RMG Research (48%-42% in mid-October) and internal surveys showing double-digit edges amid solid early voting turnout favoring Republicans. No major shifts have emerged from the sole October debate or national tailwinds boosting House GOP control prospects, with forecasts from 538 and Cook Political Report rating it Likely Republican ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Texas's 33rd Congressional District, a Democratic stronghold with a strong partisan lean, trader consensus heavily favors the incumbent Democrat Marc Veasey at 91.5% implied probability, reflecting his dominant past performances—winning by 45 points in 2022 amid a D+27 Cook rating—and minimal challenge from Republican nominee Rajesh Gopal, a political newcomer with limited fundraising and name recognition. Recent GOP primary consolidation behind Gopal failed to generate momentum, and no polls show competitive movement. Scenarios to shift odds include a major Veasey scandal, unprecedented Republican turnout surge, or court-ordered redistricting, though historical base rates in safe seats make these low-likelihood catalysts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the TX-36 House race strongly favors the Republican Party at 87.5%, driven by the district's deep-red partisan lean—rated R+24 by the Cook Political Report—and incumbent Rep. Randy Weber's entrenched position after winning his primary convincingly against Jonathan Spell in March. Southeast Texas areas like Galveston and Brazoria counties have delivered GOP margins exceeding 40 points in recent cycles, bolstering Weber's fundraising edge over Democratic challenger Robert Arnold. Recent FEC filings confirm Weber's dominance, with no polling shifts or scandals altering the outlook; early voting begins October 21, but absent surprises, the race remains non-competitive, anchoring the lopsided odds.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Burgess Owens holds a commanding lead in Utah's 4th Congressional District, a reliably red seat with a partisan voting index of R+12, driving trader consensus to 90% for the GOP in the 2024 House race. Owens secured his primary on June 25 with 75% of the vote, facing Democrat Kim Burningham, who won her nomination but trails significantly in early polling and fundraising—Owens raised over $1 million compared to Burningham's $100,000. Recent Cook Political Report ratings affirm "Solid Republican" status, unchanged by national headwinds, as Utah's conservative base and Owens' incumbency solidify low Democratic odds at 8.5%. Upcoming general election ballots in November pose minimal upset risk absent major shifts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Rep. Robert C. "Bobby" Scott's entrenched incumbency since 1993 drives the 91% trader consensus favoring Democrats in Virginia's 3rd Congressional District House race, a solidly blue seat with a D+16 partisan lean encompassing urban Norfolk and Hampton Roads. Scott cruised through an uncontested Democratic primary on June 18, while Republican nominee Mike Smith faces steep fundraising and name-recognition deficits amid sparse polling echoing FiveThirtyEight's 92% Scott win probability. Recent quiet on the trail reinforces this stability, with markets pricing in minimal upset risk absent a national GOP wave, Scott scandal, or turnout anomaly—scenarios lacking current evidence but worth monitoring ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket prices a Democratic Party win in Virginia's 5th Congressional District House race at 70.5%, driven primarily by fallout from the razor-thin Republican primary where state Sen. John McGuire edged incumbent Rep. Bob Good by just 358 votes in June 2024. Good's ouster as House Freedom Caucus chair and delayed endorsement of McGuire on July 10 have fueled trader doubts about GOP unity in the R+7 district. Democratic nominee Missy Cotter Smasal, who garnered 36% in 2022, benefits from stronger recent fundraising and national headwinds for Republicans. Absent public general election polls, markets reflect perceived voter backlash risks ahead of November balloting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in Virginia's 6th Congressional District House race tilts slightly toward Democrats at 51%, driven by recent internal and public polls showing challenger Ken Mitchell competitive against Republican incumbent Ben Cline, despite the district's R+8 partisan lean from 2020 results. Dynamics keeping it tight include Mitchell's fundraising edge—over $1 million raised versus Cline's incumbency and local name recognition—bolstered by suburban outreach in Roanoke and Charlottesville exurbs amid national anti-incumbent sentiment. Early voting trends show balanced GOP turnout, but Democratic absentee gains add uncertainty. Key separators: an October 15 debate, Trump or Harris campaign visits, or fresh October polling shifts could widen the gap.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Dan Newhouse's decisive August primary win over Trump-backed challengers has solidified GOP control in Washington’s 4th Congressional District, a reliably conservative area with a strong Republican lean reflected in past election results like his 2022 66% victory. Trader consensus at 85.5% for Republicans stems from the district's rural, eastern Washington base—rated Solid Republican by nonpartisan forecasters—and a weak Democratic nominee, Bette Rembold, facing long odds amid limited fundraising and no competitive polling. Recent developments, including steady national GOP momentum and absent late-campaign shifts, maintain this implied probability, though early voting trends could introduce minor volatility ahead of November 5.

question: Will the Democratic Party win the WA-04 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the WA-04 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the WA-05 House race strongly favors the Republican nominee at 76.5%, driven by the district's consistent Republican lean—rated R+6 by Cook PVI and carried by Trump by 10 points in 2020—paired with Jerrod Sessler's dominant primary win and polling leads of 10-15 points in recent surveys from RMG Research and internal campaigns. Incumbent Rep. Cathy McMorris Rodgers' retirement created an open seat, but Democrat Carmela Conley's fundraising disadvantages and weaker name recognition have limited her traction. Post-August primaries, no major catalysts have emerged to shift dynamics ahead of November balloting, sustaining GOP momentum amid national House control battles.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Marie Gluesenkamp Perez's commanding lead in recent polls drives the 92% trader consensus for a Democratic victory in Washington's 6th Congressional District House race. Polls from early October, including a Northwest Progressive Institute survey showing Perez ahead 51%-38% against Republican state Sen. Drew MacEwen, underscore her incumbency advantage, superior fundraising, and 2022 narrow win despite the district's slight Republican lean. Trader sentiment reflects her strengthened position amid weak GOP primary turnout and national headwinds for Republicans. Realistic challenges include a late GOP mobilization surge, unforeseen scandals, or shifts in voter turnout, though current evidence suggests low probability before Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Pramila Jayapal holds a commanding lead in Washington's 7th congressional district, a deep-blue Seattle-area seat with a D+34 partisan lean, driving trader consensus at 92.5% for the Democratic Party. Recent polls show Jayapal ahead by 40-50 points over Republican Craig Goodwin, bolstered by her fundraising edge, unopposed primary win, and consistent safe Democratic ratings from forecasters like Cook Political Report. Voter registration heavily favors Democrats, and 2020 results saw Biden win by 60 points. Realistic challenges include a major scandal targeting Jayapal, depressed Democratic turnout in a low-salience race, or a national Republican wave, though district history makes upsets rare. Traders price in minimal GOP pickup odds absent such catalysts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Rep. Adam Smith’s long incumbency and WA-09’s strong Democratic lean (D+23 Cook PVI) drive the 92.5% trader consensus for a Democratic win, reinforced by his dominant August primary performance (74% vote share) against Republican Guy Bauer. The district’s diverse suburban-urban mix south of Seattle favors Democrats on key issues like defense policy—Smith chairs the Armed Services Committee—and fundraising edges (Smith raised over $1M vs. Bauer’s $100K). No recent polls show a competitive race, aligning with historical safe-seat patterns. Realistic challenges include a major Smith scandal, national Republican wave, or voter turnout surge, though evidence suggests low probability amid stable conditions.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Marilyn Strickland's dominant position in the WA-10 congressional race drives the 90.5% trader consensus for a Democratic win, fueled by her 2022 victory margin exceeding 20 points in this D+8 leaning Puget Sound district. Recent polling averages show her leading challenger Erik Carlson by double digits, bolstered by superior fundraising—over $1.5 million cash on hand versus Carlson's under $200,000—and nonpartisan ratings labeling the seat Likely Democratic. Low GOP enthusiasm in Washington state further solidifies odds. Realistic challenges include a major Strickland scandal or national Republican wave boosting turnout, though historical base rates in similar districts suggest slim odds of reversal before November's general election.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Mark Pocan holds a commanding lead in Wisconsin's 2nd Congressional District (WI-02), a reliably blue seat with a D+24 partisan voting index, where he has won prior elections by margins exceeding 50 points. Recent FEC filings show Pocan vastly outpacing Republican challenger Waymon Smith in fundraising, while sparse polling and historical data reinforce trader consensus at 91% for Democrats. The district's liberal core around Madison faces minimal competitive pressure absent a GOP surge. Realistic challenges include an unforeseen scandal, health issue for Pocan, or anomalous turnout shifts, though these remain low-probability scenarios given the race's entrenched dynamics.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democratic Rep. Gwen Moore's entrenched dominance in Wisconsin's 4th Congressional District, a Milwaukee-heavy seat with a D+27 partisan lean, anchors trader consensus at 93.5% for Democrats. Moore's landslide reelection history—72% in 2022, 76% in 2020—combined with her unchallenged August primary win and the Republican nominee Chris Kuehl's limited fundraising and name recognition sustain this lopsided pricing. No recent polls show competitiveness, reflecting all major forecasters' "Safe Democratic" ratings. Realistic challenges include a major Moore scandal, unforeseen voter turnout surge, or broader Republican national momentum, though each remains low-probability amid stable district fundamentals.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Scott Fitzgerald's dominant position in Wisconsin's 5th Congressional District, a reliably conservative area with a partisan voter index of R+13, drives the 86.5% trader consensus for a GOP win in the House election. Fitzgerald, seeking a sixth term, cruised through an unopposed primary and secured past victories by wide margins exceeding 30 points. The Democratic nominee, relative newcomer Joe Wirth, trails significantly in available metrics, with no recent polls indicating a competitive race—forecasters like Cook Political Report rate it Solid Republican. National GOP momentum in Midwest battlegrounds further bolsters sentiment, though general election ballots close November 5 could introduce minor shifts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. Glenn Grothman's consistent polling edge over Democratic challenger Jeff Zinnen anchors trader consensus at 85% for a Republican hold in Wisconsin's 6th Congressional District. Recent aggregates from Race to the WH show Grothman leading 52.5% to 46.5%, a +6 margin echoing his 2022 double-digit victory in the R+5 leaning district. No major catalysts have emerged to erode this advantage, including steady fundraising leads for Grothman and limited national Democratic investment in the race. Upcoming early voting and final debates could influence sentiment, but current wisdom-of-crowds pricing reflects entrenched incumbent strength amid stable midterm dynamics.

eventMetadata.context_description: Republican incumbent Derrick Van Orden's consistent double-digit leads in recent polls anchor the 85.5% trader consensus for a GOP hold in Wisconsin's 7th Congressional District House race. The district's R+4 partisan lean and Van Orden's 2022 victory margin of nearly 4 points provide a strong baseline, with challenger Tom Buettner trailing 10-15 points in surveys from RMG Research and others through early October. No major shifts have emerged from fundraising edges favoring Van Orden or early voting trends, reinforcing market stability amid national Republican momentum in battleground House seats. Upcoming absentee ballot data could influence sentiment, but current evidence points to low Democratic upset risk.

eventMetadata.context_description: The decisive Republican victory in Wisconsin's 8th Congressional District special election on August 13, where Tony Wied won 62% to Democrat Ryan Solen's 38%, has solidified trader consensus at 80% odds for a GOP hold in the November general election. This open seat, vacated by retiring Rep. Mike Gallagher, features Republican Brad Schimel, a former state attorney general, leading polls by 10-15 points over Democrat Kristin Ohlrogge amid a district that leans Republican (Cook PVI R+4). Strong GOP fundraising and base turnout in the primary further bolster probabilities, though national House dynamics and late shifts remain wildcards ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus overwhelmingly favors the Republican candidate at 93.5% implied probability in West Virginia's 1st Congressional District House race, anchored by incumbent Carol Miller's dominant position in a solidly Republican district with a Cook Partisan Voting Index of R+32. Miller cruised through the May 2024 GOP primary with 74% of the vote, bolstered by superior fundraising—over $500,000 raised versus Democrat James Murray's modest $20,000—and consistent landslide victories in prior cycles, including 68% in 2022. West Virginia's rightward shift under Trump further entrenches GOP strength here. Realistic challenges remain slim but could arise from an unforeseen Miller scandal, depressed Republican turnout amid national dynamics, or a surprise Democratic mobilization, though historical base rates suggest negligible risk ahead of the November 5 general election.

events.title: Google Gemini score on Humanity’s Last Exam by June 30?

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket assigns low implied probabilities to Google Gemini achieving a meaningful score on Humanity’s Last Exam—a grueling 1,000-question benchmark of expert-level problems designed to probe AGI frontiers—by June 30, driven by current models' dismal single-digit performances, with Gemini 1.5 Pro lagging at around 4-5% per recent Scale AI leaderboard updates. No official Google announcements signal an imminent capability leap, despite multimodal enhancements teased at I/O, amid fierce competition from Claude 3.5 Sonnet (topping ~7%) and OpenAI's o1-preview reasoning boosts. Traders eye potential last-minute Gemini 2.0 previews or surprise evals, but historical benchmark slippage tempers optimism before the hard deadline.

question: Map Handicap: 3DMAX (-1.5) vs FUT Esports (+1.5)
description:
This market refers to the Counter-Strike match between FUT Esports and 3DMAX in the IEM Krakow Play-In, scheduled for January 28 at 10:35AM ET.

This market will resolve to "3DMAX" if 3DMAX wins 2 or more maps than FUT Esports in this match.

Otherwise, this market will resolve to "FUT Esports".

If the match is canceled (not played at all), ends in a tie, or is delayed beyond 7 days from the scheduled date without a winner determined, this market will resolve to 50-50. 

If the match begins but is not completed, and one team wins due to the opponent's forfeiture, disqualification, or walkover, this market will resolve to 50-50. 

If the match ends in a forfeit, disqualification, or walkover (team withdraws before the start and the other wins automatically), this market will resolve to 50-50.

events.title: Counter-Strike: FUT Esports vs 3DMAX (BO3) - IEM Krakow Play-In
events.description:
This market refers to the Counter-Strike match between FUT Esports and 3DMAX in the IEM Krakow Play-In, scheduled for January 28 at 6:00AM ET.

This market will resolve to "FUT Esports" if FUT Esports win the match against 3DMAX.

This market will resolve to "3DMAX" if 3DMAX win the match against FUT Esports.

If the match is canceled (not played at all), ends in a tie, or is delayed beyond 7 days from the scheduled date without a winner determined, this market will resolve to 50-50. 

If the match begins but is not completed, and one team wins due to the opponent's forfeiture, disqualification, or walkover, this market will resolve to the team who wins. 

If the match ends in a forfeit, disqualification, or walkover (team withdraws before the start and the other wins automatically), this market will resolve to 50-50. 

The resolution source for this market will be official information from https://www.sofascore.com/esports.

question: Will the Bank of Canada announce a 50+ bps decrease at the April meeting?
description:
This market will resolve to the amount of basis points the target for the overnight rate is changed by versus the level it was prior to the Bank of Canada's April 2026 meeting.

If the target for the overnight rate is changed to a level not expressed in the displayed options, the change will be rounded up to the nearest 25 basis points and will resolve to the relevant bracket. For example, if the relevant rate is increased or decreased by 12.5 basis points, it will be treated as a 25 basis point change for the purposes of resolution.

The resolution source for this market is information released by the Bank of Canada after its April 29, 2026 policy meeting, as listed on the official Bank of Canada calendar: https://www.bankofcanada.ca/core-functions/monetary-policy/key-interest-rate/#target-dates

This market may resolve as soon as the Bank of Canada's statement for their April meeting with relevant data is issued. If no statement is released by the end date of the next scheduled meeting, this market will resolve to the "No change" bracket.

This market may resolve as soon as the Bank of Canada's statement for their April meeting with relevant data is issued. If no statement is released by the end date of the next scheduled meeting, this market will resolve to the "No change" bracket.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket overwhelmingly favors no change in the Bank of Canada's April policy rate at 93% implied probability, driven by sticky core inflation metrics hovering around 3%—above the 2% target—despite headline CPI easing to 2.9% in February. Recent labor data shows unemployment ticking up to 6.1%, but resilient GDP growth and Governor Macklem's data-dependent stance from the March hold at 5% reinforce expectations of steady policy amid balanced risks. A realistic challenge would require surprisingly weak jobs or CPI prints prompting an early cut, though traders price such downside at under 4%; hotter inflation could marginally boost hike odds but remains improbable given cooling trends.

question: Iran agrees to end enrichment of uranium by March 31?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if Iran publicly agrees to end all enrichment of uranium by March 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”.

An official pledge by Iran to end all enrichment of Uranium will qualify for a “Yes” resolution whether as a unilateral announcement or part of an agreement with the U.S. or Israel.

Any agreement or pledge made before the resolution date of this market will qualify, regardless of if/when the agreement goes into effect.

An agreement by Iran to end all enrichment of uranium for any amount of time will count.

An agreement by Iran to end all enrichment of uranium as a precondition of a more comprehensive peace process or deal will qualify, even if the agreement is not finalized or part of a formalized peace deal. 

Agreements to merely limit or cap the level or quality of enrichment—such as reducing enrichment to below weapons-grade thresholds—will not qualify.

The primary resolution source for this market will be a consensus of credible reporting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Iran's steadfast refusal to curb its uranium enrichment program, affirmed by Supreme Leader Khamenei's recent statements rejecting negotiations under sanctions pressure, underpins the 97.8% trader consensus against any agreement by March 31. IAEA reports confirm Iran's stockpile surpassing 6,200 kg of enriched uranium—much at 60% purity nearing weapons-grade—with new advanced centrifuges installed despite June's censure vote. Absent diplomatic breakthroughs like renewed JCPOA talks or sanctions relief incentives, traders see negligible upside risk. Realistic shifts could stem from U.S. election outcomes prompting indirect Vienna channel revivals or Israeli strikes forcing de-escalation, though historical deadlocks favor status quo persistence.

question: Zhang Youxia sentenced to prison before 2027?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if Chinese General Zhang Youxia is sentenced to any time in jail/prison for any charges by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

This market will remain open through December 31, 2026 or until Zhang Youxia is sentenced to time in jail/prison, regardless of any initial sentencing that does not include prison. Any sentencing will qualify, regardless of subsequent appeals or changes to the sentence.

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from the Chinese government and law enforcement; however a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Zhang Youxia's entrenched position as vice chairman of China's Central Military Commission and close alliance with Xi Jinping underpin the strong trader consensus against a pre-2027 prison sentence, with "No" shares implying 82.5% probability. Recent military anti-corruption drives have ensnared figures like former defense ministers Li Shangfu and Wei Fenghe, but official state media portrays Zhang actively leading reforms and attending high-level events alongside Xi, such as July 2024 military inspections. No credible reports indicate investigations targeting him, and his revolutionary lineage bolsters perceived immunity amid PLA purges focused on lower ranks. Traders weigh this stability against the seismic political fallout of indicting a top uniformed leader before the 2027 Party Congress.

question: Will John Fetterman vote "Yea" on the Department of Homeland Security Appropriations Act, 2026?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the listed U.S. senator votes "Yea" on passage of the Department of Homeland Security Appropriations Act, 2026, during the first roll-call vote on passage in the U.S. Senate. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

This market will resolve to "No" if no vote on passage of the Department of Homeland Security Appropriations Act, 2026, occurs by March 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET.

The listed senator's vote during the first qualifying roll-call vote on passage of the Department of Homeland Security Appropriations Act, 2026, will be used for this market’s resolution, regardless of whether that bill is later signed into law.

Any vote by the listed U.S. senator on the Department of Homeland Security Appropriations Act, 2026, whether that bill is voted on individually or as part of a larger legislative package in which DHS appropriations are clearly included, will qualify for this market’s resolution.

Any vote on a continuing resolution (CR), whether or not it includes Department of Homeland Security funding, will not qualify for this market’s resolution.

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from the U.S. Government. However, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment on Yea votes for the DHS Appropriations Act 2026, due by March 31, centers on bipartisan negotiations to fund homeland security amid fiscal pressures and shutdown risks. Recent House Appropriations Committee passage of a $60 billion version last week advanced the bill, aligning with GOP priorities on border enforcement while drawing Democratic pushback over cuts to immigration services. Whip counts show solid Republican support, but moderate defections loom if Senate Democrats amend for higher spending. Upcoming full House floor vote next week and potential conference reconciliation could sway outcomes, with market odds reflecting members' records on prior DHS bills and leadership arm-twisting. Uncertainty persists given partisan divides.

Recent House Appropriations Committee passage of a $60 billion version last week advanced the bill, aligning with GOP priorities on border enforcement while drawing Democratic pushback over cuts to immigration services. Whip counts show solid Republican support, but moderate defections loom if Senate Democrats amend for higher spending. Upcoming full House floor vote next week and potential conference reconciliation could sway outcomes, with market odds reflecting members' records on prior DHS bills and leadership arm-twisting. Uncertainty persists given partisan divides.

question: Will Canada's drop in population in 2026 be the largest on record?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the decrease in the population of Canada from the 4th quarter of 2025 and the 4th quarter of 2026 is greater than that of any year on record. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

The resolution source for this market will be the quarterly population estimates release for the 4th quarter of 2026 from StatCan (https://www.statcan.gc.ca/en/start). 

If no data for the specified quarter is released by the date the next quarter's data is scheduled to be released, this market will resolve based on the most recent data from the last available quarter.

Note: data from the initial release of the referenced population estimate report is what will be used to resolve this market. Data may be revised during the following quarter or as a part of the next estimate's publication, however any revisions to population estimate report data made after the initial release of the specified report will not be considered for this market's resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trudeau government's October 2024 Immigration Levels Plan has slashed permanent resident targets to 380,000 for 2026—down from over 500,000 previously—while capping non-permanent residents, slowing projected population growth to near zero after 3% surges in recent years. Traders price "No" at 53.5% implied probability, reflecting Statistics Canada medium-growth forecasts still showing modest 0.1-0.3% expansion and historical precedent of no annual decline since 1957, with the record drop (-0.8% in 1931) far steeper. Competitive odds stem from 2025 federal election uncertainty: a Conservative win under Poilievre could deepen cuts for a sharper contraction (tipping Yes), while Liberal continuity or economic rebound might sustain inflows (bolstering No). Watch updated StatsCan projections and polls.

question: Another GTA VI trailer released by March 31?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if another official trailer for the next GTA (GTA VI) is released by Rockstar Games or any of its official accounts, including but not limited to Rockstar's official website, YouTube channel, Twitter, or any other official social media platform between market creation and March 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET.

An "official trailer" is defined as a video clearly labelled and marketed as a trailer for the game in question. Teasers, gameplay snippets, or other promotional materials will not qualify for resolution unless explicitly marketed as an official trailer by Rockstar Games.

This market will resolve immediately if such an official trailer is released by the expiry date. If no official trailer is released by the specified date, this market will resolve to "No".

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus strongly backs "No" at 93.2% implied probability for a second GTA VI trailer by March 31, primarily due to Rockstar Games' characteristic radio silence since the explosive December 2023 debut, with no official teases amid their secretive development playbook—historical gaps between GTA trailers often span 8-12 months, leaving ample runway to the fall 2025 launch. Take-Two Interactive's recent earnings reaffirmed the timeline without marketing hints, quelling early-year rumors fueled by fan leaks and viral speculation. While unpredictable surprise drops or leak-driven releases remain slim possibilities, the lack of promotional momentum and award-season distractions solidify trader conviction in deferral.

question: Will Russia capture Kupiansk-Vuzlovyi by March 31?
description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if, according to the ISW map, Russia captures the Kupyansjk-Vuzlovij railroad station located between Zaliznychna Vulytsia and Zaliznychnykiv Ploshcha (49.654359° N, 37.644326° E) by March 31, 2026, at 11:59 PM ET.

The railroad station will be considered captured if any part of the train station icon is shaded red on the ISW map (https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/36a7f6a6f5a9448496de641cf64bd375) by the resolution date. If the area is not shaded red by March 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, the market will resolve to “No”.

For any change on the ISW map to qualify for this market’s resolution, the relevant shading indicating Russian control must persist through the next full ISW daily update cycle. If ISW skips a day, shading must persist until the next finalized ISW update is published, regardless of the date. Any continuous shading which reflects either “Assessed Russian Control”, “Assessed Russian Advance In Ukraine”, or “Assessed Russian Gains in the Past 24 Hours” will qualify. “Assessed Russian Infiltration Areas in Ukraine” will not qualify.

If Russia comes into control of this territory as a result of a negotiated settlement, this will qualify for a 'Yes' resolution, regardless of whether it is shaded red in the ISW map. An announcement of a negotiated settlement that gives Russia de jure control will not qualify. Actual control must be established.

Once Russia captures the railroad station, any subsequent loss of control will not be considered towards the resolution of this market.

Train Station Location: https://polymarket-upload.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/Kupiansk-Vuzlovyi+1.png

Train Station Location in Kupiansk-Vuzlovyi: https://polymarket-upload.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/Kupiansk-Vuzlovyi+2.png

Kupiansk-Vuzlovyi Location: https://polymarket-upload.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/Kupiansk-Vuzlovyi+3.png

Location on Google Maps: https://maps.app.goo.gl/2DWkLwtmD3dTUGbc8

The primary resolution source for this market will be the ISW Ukraine map. If the ISW map is rendered unavailable, information from DeepStateMap (https://deepstatemap.live/) may be used. If information from both the ISW and DeepStateMap are rendered permanently unavailable, a consensus of credible reporting may be used.

Note: Any temporary glitches or errors in the map will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Russian forces' incremental advances toward Kupiansk-Vuzlovyi, a key railway junction supporting Ukrainian logistics in Kharkiv Oblast, form the primary driver of trader consensus on capture odds. Recent reports detail Russian seizure of nearby villages like Stepova Novoselivka and progress along forest belts, per Institute for the Study of War assessments, amid intensified assaults exploiting Ukrainian troop rotations and ammunition constraints. Ukrainian forces report repelling attacks with drones and artillery, maintaining defensive lines despite shortages. Traders factor in Russia's manpower edge from North Korean reinforcements against potential U.S. aid boosts. Harsh winter conditions and any major frontline shifts, such as in Kursk, loom as pivotal upcoming catalysts.

question: Abstract FDV above $200M one day after launch?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the Fully Diluted Valuation of Abstract's governance token is greater than the value specified in the title 1 day after launch. Otherwise, the market will resolve to "No."

The token must be actively, publicly transferable and tradable to be considered a launch.

The FDV will be determined using the total token supply multiplied by the token price.

"1 day after launch" is defined as 4:00 PM ET on the calendar day following launch. The resolution source for this market is the most liquid price source available. If Abstract doesn't launch a token by December 31, 2027, 11:59 PM ET, this market will resolve to "No".
